content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA containing NNN integers, and an integer KKK (1≤K≤N1 \leq K \leq N1≤K≤N).

Find the number of subarrays of AAA with length KKK whose bitwise OR is odd.

**Note:** A subarray of AAA is a contiguous segment of elements of AAA.

For example, if A=[1,3,2]A = [1, 3, 2]A=[1,3,2], then it has 666 non-empty subarrays: [1],[3],[2],[1,3],[3,2],[1,3,2][1], [3], [2], [1, 3], [3, 2], [1, 3, 2][1],[3],[2],[1,3],[3,2],[1,3,2].

In particular, [1,2][1, 2][1,2] is not a subarray of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of the array and the subarray size you have to check, respectively.
The second line of each test contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of length-KKK subarrays of AAA whose bitwise OR is odd.
constraints: 1 ≤ N ≤ 5*10^5 (sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5*10^5)
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines of input. The first line contains two space-separated integers N and K — the length of the array and the subarray size you have to check, respectively. The second line of each test contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN — the elements of the array.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the number of length-K subarrays of A whose bitwise OR is odd.

sampleTestCases.explanation: First test case windows of size 2: [5,7] OR=7 (odd), [7,13] OR=15 (odd), [13,4] OR=13 (odd), [4,6] OR=6 (even) → 3 odd windows. Second test case windows of size 3: [2,6,7] OR=7 (odd), [6,7,4] OR=7 (odd) → 2.
First case all numbers are even, so every window has even OR → 0. Second case windows: [1,2,4] contains odd → odd OR, [2,4,6] all even → even OR → answer 1.

solution.approach: The key insight is to use a sliding window approach to efficiently calculate the bitwise OR of all subarrays of length K. We maintain a variable to keep track of the number of subarrays with an odd bitwise OR. For each subarray, we calculate its bitwise OR and check if it's odd. If it is, we increment the count.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) per test case, O(T * N) total. The precomputation is done once for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the input array A.
